- [WA] Spanning-tree graph: assumes one DFS/BFS tree preserves the structure that matters and all non-tree edges are local bonuses. The wrong shape runs tree DP, rerooting, subtree merges, or LCA tricks on a chosen spanning tree, then patches a few chords. It stays plausible after picking a maximum-degree tree or "important" root, but fails when one chord couples distant subtree states, parity, or witness obligations.
- [WA] `one_edge_structural_flip`: pass a near-miss tree, DAG, cactus, bridge, or bipartite check, then continue as if the structural promise were exact. The wrong shape is often one controlled edge away from the real theorem: a back edge changes topological semantics, one chord changes tree uniqueness, or one odd/cross edge changes the matching or parity model.
- [WA] `dfs_bfs_witness_portfolio`: treat the first DFS/BFS parent tree, topological witness, shortest witness, or representative path as canonical when several are locally equivalent. The shortcut survives because each individual witness is valid for reachability, but downstream constraints can distinguish which witness, port, parity, or edge order was chosen.
- [WA/TLE] Low-cycle or cactus shortcut: assumes the graph is a tree plus a small independent cycle basis, so each cycle can be handled alone. The wrong shape solves cactus blocks exactly, enumerates retained chords, or caps extra edges after a threshold; legal biconnected pieces with many interacting cycles either invalidate the dropped interactions or make the retained extras exponential.
- [WA] Topological DP after cycle pruning: assumes directed cycles only add redundant reachability and a partial order still drives the answer. The wrong shape runs Kahn/DFS order, ignores leftover cyclic vertices, deletes back edges, or processes the residual once in arbitrary order. It breaks when the internal SCC choice controls which exit, resource, lexicographic witness, or parity state survives.
- [WA] One-summary SCC condensation: assumes every vertex inside an SCC is interchangeable after condensation. The wrong shape stores size, best score, parity, "has special vertex", or one entry/exit pair per component and then does DP on the condensation DAG. Extra scalars still fail when different incoming edges require different internal paths or unlock different outgoing edges.
- [WA/TLE] Exact-inside-small-SCC heuristic: assumes large SCCs are rare or internally uniform, so only small components need real solving. The wrong shape brute-forces or runs expensive local DP inside SCCs below a threshold and summarizes the rest; one large asymmetric SCC gives WA, while many just-below-threshold SCCs force the expensive branch everywhere.
- [WA] Block-tree overcompression: assumes bridges, articulation points, and biconnected components reduce the graph to a tree with one local value per block. The wrong shape builds the bridge tree or block-cut tree and merges block summaries as if attachment ports were equivalent. Keeping top-k local candidates or parity variants per block is still insufficient when entering through one articulation changes the best exit or witness inside the block.
- [WA] Cactus-style block handling on general biconnected pieces: assumes every block behaves like one simple cycle or a path with one detour. The wrong shape treats dense blocks as a representative cycle, a DFS ear, or a shortest local detour, then feeds that into a tree DP. It fails when two cycles share internal vertices or when useful routes must choose among non-equivalent ports in the same block.
- [WA] Boundary-forgetting subgraph summaries: assumes a side of a cut, separator, SCC, or block can be represented by best value, size, parity, or feasibility alone. The wrong shape solves each piece locally and merges without remembering which boundary vertices are used, connected, or still available. Two histories with the same scalar summary can require incompatible boundary behavior later.
- [WA/TLE/MLE] `auxiliary_graph_compression`: build a condensation graph, bridge tree, block-cut tree, state graph, quotient graph, or flow/matching auxiliary graph, then assume the auxiliary object preserves equality, multiplicity, direction, capacity, alternating structure, and boundary identity. Exact auxiliary algorithms do not justify dropping payload that the original problem still observes, and deduplicating the wrong edge or port can switch between WA and memory blowup.
- [TLE/MLE/WA] `sparse_dense_switching`: branch between sparse and dense algorithms, adjacency representations, transitive closure, or reduced-state solvers using a density threshold that was measured on friendly data rather than proved for the generated auxiliary object. Sparse original graphs can create dense closures or wide frontiers, while dense-looking reductions can still need multiplicity or witness identity that the sparse branch discards.
- [WA] Flow or matching overconfidence: assumes a polished max-flow, min-cut, bipartite matching, or Hall-style reduction is exact under weaker hypotheses than the theorem needs. The wrong shape omits vertex splitting, lower-bound feasibility, connectivity side constraints, odd-cycle effects, colors, ordering, or witness shape. Local repair passes and tie-order changes do not fix a model that forgot a real constraint.
- [WA/TLE] `greedy_matching_flow_repair`: start from a greedy matching, maximal flow-like witness, or local cover, then run bounded swaps, local reroutes, or residual repairs until it appears stable. Random graphs leave plenty of slack; Hall-tight, cut-tight, or alternating-structure cases need a global augmenting or blocking argument rather than a repair radius.
- [WA/TLE] `crown_matching_order_trap`: rely on Kuhn-style, BFS-style, or degree-ordered augmentation whose performance and chosen matching depend on the input vertex and edge order. The named risk belongs in graphs whenever the problem is a general graph task with a matching-flavored subroutine, even if detailed modeling failures live in the matching/flow topic.
- [WA/TLE] `weak_dinic_layered_graph`: keep Dinic/Hopcroft-Karp vocabulary but weaken the phase by sending only one path, stopping before the level graph is blocked, or carrying stale current-arc state across incompatible residual layers. Unit-network bounds and exact blocking-flow proofs do not transfer to reductions with capacities, side constraints, or partially drained phases.
- [TLE/MLE] Heavy reduction per query: assumes rebuilding and rerunning full flow, min-cut, or matching for every update, root, candidate answer, or binary-search probe is acceptable. Dinic, Hopcroft-Karp, and residual-network copies pass small batches, then collapse under `q * flow` or `q * matching`; adversarial updates can invalidate the cached part that looked reusable.
- [TLE/WA] Unit-network optimism in flow reductions: assumes the faster unit-network or matching-style bound still applies after adding capacities, lower bounds, vertex gadgets, or side constraints. The wrong shape uses Dinic as if it were always `O(E sqrt V)`, or strips capacities to recover that bound. On the actual constructed network it either solves the wrong problem or falls back to the general `O(V^2 E)` scale.
- [WA] Bipartite-by-appearance: assumes labels, DFS depth parity, or sample construction provide a valid bipartition. The wrong shape colors greedily, sends conflicts to one side, or deletes a few conflict edges before matching/cut DP. An almost-bipartite graph with one odd cycle changes the theorem being used.
- [WA] Greedy augmenting or degree-ordered matching: assumes local degree pressure exposes the global obstruction. The wrong shape repeatedly matches lowest-degree or highest-gain vertices, maybe with deterministic restarts and local swaps. It remains vulnerable to tight Hall subsets and alternating structures where every local choice looks safe until the final deficit appears.
- [WA] DSU used for non-equivalence: assumes reachability, directed connectivity, or a changing graph can be represented by undirected monotone components. The wrong shape unions edges as they appear, treats strongly and weakly connected vertices alike, or ignores deletions once a merge happened. Checkpoint rebuilds still fail when direction, time order, or a deleted bridge matters.
- [WA/TLE] Dynamic graph recomputation: assumes recomputing components, SCCs, bridges, or articulation points after each operation is acceptable because updates look sparse. The wrong shape is correct per snapshot but costs `O(q(n+m))`; partial caching often goes stale when one update changes a DFS ancestor, lowlink, or bridge status far from the edited edge.
- [TLE] Incremental graph maintenance with local dirty regions: assumes each update has local impact. The wrong shape repairs affected vertices, DFS ancestors, SCC summaries, or reachability labels near the changed edge. A bridge near the root or one edge between dense regions can make the dirty region expand to the whole graph on every operation.
- [TLE] Per-source traversal for global reachability: assumes BFS/DFS from every vertex, query endpoint, candidate root, or color class is close enough on sparse random graphs. Layered DAGs, long chains with many query sources, or dense reachable regions force the worst-case `O(k(n+m))` even when pruning and memoized visited stamps look effective.
- [TLE/MLE] All-pairs shortest paths or reachability on sparse limits: assumes Floyd-Warshall, repeated Dijkstra, repeated BFS, or a full distance matrix is harmless because the graph is not dense in samples. Large sparse legal graphs make `O(n^3)`, `O(nm log n)`, or `n^2` storage dominate before the graph-specific idea is tested.
- [TLE/MLE] Transitive closure by bitsets without a budget proof: assumes `n^2 / word_size` memory and bitwise propagation time are acceptable because the graph is a DAG or condensation DAG. Wide layered graphs, many SCC components, or many OR-heavy edges make both the bitset table and the OR traffic hit limits.
- [TLE/MLE] Frontier propagation assumes wavefronts stay thin. The wrong shape keeps exact queues, distance layers, or color-reachability sets for every active source, then relies on overlap to merge cheaply. Layered or expander-like graphs can make every layer wide while sources remain distinguishable, so sketches or color buckets save memory only by losing witnesses.
- [MLE] Dense adjacency or pair-state representation for sparse limits: assumes adjacency, reachability, compatibility, or pair DP can be stored as `n x n` because edge checks become simple. Coordinate compression helps only if it reduces the worst-case vertex set; sparse legal graphs with large `n` expose the resource failure while `m` remains small.
- [TLE/MLE] Condensation graph edge blow-up: assumes compressing SCCs or blocks also makes the edge set small. Keeping every original cross edge, every parallel component edge, or a component-pair matrix can still cost `O(m)` or `O(c^2)` after compression. Deduplicating too aggressively becomes WA when multiplicity, capacity, or witness count matters.
- [WA/TLE/MLE] Shortest-path label collapse: assumes one best distance per vertex is enough even when the statement has stops, coupons, colors, forbidden resources, parity, or required witnesses. If the missing state is ignored it is WA; if all state is restored blindly, the state graph can grow by `k`, a product of resources, or a mask dimension and become the real TLE/MLE.
- [WA] Dijkstra outside its invariant: assumes nonnegative-weight shortest-path reasoning survives negative edges, path-dependent costs, or subtractive rewards. Reweighting by a constant per edge, stopping when the target is first popped, or marking a vertex final too early breaks when a later path can become cheaper for a reason not represented in the key.
- [TLE/MLE] Priority-queue shortest path with stale entries kept alive: assumes duplicate heap states are just a constant-factor nuisance. Without a strict stale-distance discard, repeated relaxations on dense or layered graphs can grow toward Bellman-Ford-like `O(nm)` behavior and keep a large heap even though the mathematical shortest-path algorithm would be fine.
- [TLE/RE] Label-correcting shortest path optimism: assumes SPFA or D'Esopo-Pape is near-linear because road-like samples are friendly. Queue order tweaks, SLF/LLL, and deques do not remove worst-case counterexamples; with negative cycles or missing relaxation counters the same shape can also fail to terminate.
- [RE] Recursive DFS/Tarjan/Dinic on deep legal graphs: assumes recursion depth follows random graph height. A path, comb, layered residual network, or chain of components within input limits can overflow before the algorithmic idea is otherwise challenged.
- [WA] Multigraph simplification: assumes parallel edges and self-loops are harmless duplicates because the conceptual graph is simple. The wrong shape inserts edges into sets, drops self-loops, or counts a pair once before bridge, cut, parity, degree, capacity, or walk reasoning. Multiplicity can change bridge status, degree constraints, cycle parity, capacity, or witness counts.
- [WA] Parent-vertex lowlink shortcut: assumes skipping the parent vertex once is equivalent to skipping the incoming edge. Bridge and articulation code that does not track edge identity can misclassify parallel edges or self-loops, especially when the statement allows multigraphs but samples look simple.
- [WA] Reverse-graph symmetry shortcut: assumes in-neighborhood and out-neighborhood summaries are interchangeable. The wrong shape computes only totals, one forward reachability pass, or one reverse pass and reuses it for the opposite direction. Sources and sinks can share counts while having different reachable witnesses or constraints.
- [WA] First-witness pruning in cyclic search: assumes the first found path, parent, incoming edge, augmenting path, or representative vertex is as good as any other. The wrong shape marks vertices permanently after one discovery and discards later alternatives reaching the same vertex with different state. Two or three representatives per vertex still fail when the missing distinction is boundary port, parity, budget, or used-edge set.
- [WA] Degree-local greedy: assumes high degree, low degree, leafiness, or local cut size reveals the globally correct vertices or edges. The wrong shape peels leaves, chooses hubs, contracts low-degree regions, or breaks ties by local score before applying exact logic to the reduced graph. Regular gadgets and delayed bottlenecks keep local statistics plausible while changing the optimum.
- [WA] k-core or leaf-peeling overreach: assumes removed leaves, low-core vertices, or peripheral components cannot participate in the decisive witness. The wrong shape repeatedly prunes vertices below a threshold and solves only the remaining core, maybe adding a cheap correction. It fails when the core choice depends on a tree tail, pruned articulation path, or low-degree vertex carrying a required label/resource.
- [TLE/MLE/WA] Simple-path or cycle enumeration with optimistic pruning: assumes the number of relevant paths or cycles is small after bounding length, avoiding revisits, or stopping after top-k candidates. Complete, layered, and strongly cyclic graphs can contain factorially or exponentially many near-identical candidates; caps create WA when the unique valid witness sits beyond the cap.
- [TLE/MLE/WA] Separator, cut, or articulation-choice enumeration: assumes few near-minimum structures need consideration. Branching on cut vertices, bridge replacements, or separator sides is plausible in tree-like samples, but dense equal-capacity and cactus-like graphs can have too many incomparable cuts. Explicit storage explodes; capping the family loses correctness.
- [WA/TLE] Component-local exact solve with greedy global merge: assumes components, SCCs, or blocks can be solved exactly and then combined by one scalar ordering. The wrong shape sorts local answers by value, density, size, or a "best mode" and takes what fits. Keeping cheap/expensive or parity-flipped modes remains wrong when the real objective couples several medium components through a tight budget or global witness rule.
- [WA/MLE] Underpowered global DP over graph pieces: assumes cross-component coupling is captured by component count, total size, one parity bit, or one budget dimension. The wrong shape performs a small knapsack over summaries from exact local solvers and discards boundary identities or witness classes. Restoring all identities can turn into a table over component states too large for the limit.
- [WA] Connectivity monotonicity in objectives that are not monotone: assumes adding edges can only help or removing edges can only hurt. The wrong shape binary-searches a threshold, greedily adds edges, or discards "extra" constraints after feasibility appears. Extra edges can create forbidden cycles, parity flips, capacity conflicts, uniqueness violations, or different lexicographic witnesses.
